name: Pink Anemonefish
habitat: Shallow coral reefs, exclusively within host anemones
diet: Omnivore — zooplankton, algae, small invertebrates
behavior: Monogamous. Demersal spawning — males guard and aerate eggs. Annual fecundity rate of 2,000–4,000 eggs. Dominance hierarchy with female as largest.
funFact: Despite their small size, a breeding pair can produce 2,000–4,000 eggs per year — prolific little parents on the reef.

name: Saddleback Anemonefish
habitat: Shallow reefs, carpet anemones on sandy substrates
diet: Omnivore — zooplankton, benthic crustaceans and algae
behavior: Monogamous. Aggressively territorial, defending its host anemone against butterflyfish. Sequential hermaphrodite — if the female dies, the breeding male changes sex.
funFact: If the female dies, the breeding male changes sex to female, and the largest non-breeding male becomes the new breeding male.

name: Titan Triggerfish
habitat: Coral reefs, lagoons, and reef flats
diet: Sea urchins, coral, crabs, crustaceans, Crown-of-Thorns starfish and molluscs
behavior: Largest triggerfish species, up to 75cm. Hostile when caring for eggs. Strong teeth adapted to diet. Trigger fin comes up when threatened. Females commonly larger than males.
funFact: Their territory extends in a cone shape upward from the nest — swim horizontally away, not up, if one charges at you!

name: Giant Moray Eel
habitat: Coral reef crevices, caves, and overhangs
diet: Carnivore — fish, octopus, crustaceans
behavior: Nocturnal hunter. Opens and closes mouth to pump water over gills (not threatening). Can grow up to 3 meters long.
funFact: Has a second set of jaws in its throat (pharyngeal jaws) that extend forward to grab prey and pull it down, like the creature from Alien!

name: Ornate Butterflyfish
habitat: Coral-rich areas of lagoons and seaward reefs
diet: Coral polyps, primarily Pocillopora species
behavior: Usually seen in mated pairs. Highly territorial. One of the most strikingly patterned butterflyfish.
funFact: Butterflyfish are considered indicator species for reef health — their presence means the coral is thriving.

name: Copperband Butterflyfish
habitat: Rocky shores, coral reefs, estuaries
diet: Predator of benthic invertebrates — invertebrates that live on the seafloor
behavior: Territorial. Breeding pairs are monogamous — once they find their partner, they stay together for life. Oviparous.
funFact: Once they find their partner, they stay together for life — one of the most loyal fish on the reef.

name: Blue-ringed Angelfish
habitat: Coastal reefs, often near caves and crevices
diet: Omnivore — zooplankton, algae, sponges and tunicates
behavior: Lives in a harem — one male to several females. All juveniles are born female. Adults often found in pairs. Undergo complete colour change from juvenile to adult.
funFact: All juveniles are born female — and undergo a complete colour transformation when changing from juvenile to adult.

name: Steephead Parrotfish
habitat: Coral reefs, lagoons, reef slopes
diet: Herbivore — algae scraped from coral and rock
behavior: Bites off chunks of coral to access algae. Creates the white sand beaches by excreting ground-up coral.
funFact: A single parrotfish can produce up to 100kg of white sand per year! They also secrete a mucus sleeping bag at night to hide their scent from predators.

name: Banded Sea Krait
habitat: Coral reefs, rocky shores, semiaquatic
diet: Carnivore — eels and small fish, swallowed whole
behavior: Venomous — paralyses prey. Semiaquatic. Females significantly larger. Paddle-like tail evolved for swimming and doubles as a decoy. Adults split time equally between land and sea.
funFact: Their paddle-like tail doubles as a decoy — predators mistake it for the head, giving the krait time to escape.

name: Yellowtail Barracuda
habitat: Sheltered tropical reefs, mid-water
diet: Carnivore — small fish and squid
behavior: Found in schools for protection. Safety in numbers and tornado swimming to confuse predators. Attracted to shiny objects.
funFact: Attracted to shiny, reflective objects — divers are advised to remove jewellery before entering barracuda territory.

name: Pickhandle Barracuda
habitat: Mid-water in coastal bays and around reef structures
diet: Carnivore — mainly fishes, sometimes squid
behavior: Solitary or small groups as adults, juveniles form larger groups. Migratory. Nocturnal hunters. Dioecism with external fertilisation.
funFact: Named for the dark bar markings on their sides resembling pickaxe handles — at 1.5 metres, the largest commonly schooling barracuda.

name: Yellowback Fusilier
habitat: Mid-water above coral reefs, open water near drop-offs
diet: Planktivore — zooplankton
behavior: Forms large, fast-moving schools above the reef. Key prey species that supports larger predators.
funFact: They change color dramatically — bright blue and yellow during the day, turning reddish at night as camouflage.

name: Whale Shark
habitat: Open water, often near plankton-rich upwellings
diet: Filter feeder — planktonic and nektonic prey, feeds by suction gulping
behavior: Largest known extant fish species, up to 18m. Skin up to 15cm thick. Highly migratory but returns to same sites annually. Ovoviviparous with litters of over 300 pups.
funFact: Each whale shark has a unique pattern of spots — like a fingerprint — used by scientists worldwide to identify and track individuals.

name: Cobia
habitat: Open water, near structures like pinnacles and wrecks
diet: Carnivore — crabs, fishes and squid
behavior: Solitary or in small groups. Pelagic. Follows larger animals to scavenge. Pelagic spawners. Closest relative is the shark sucker (remora).
funFact: Its closest living relative is the remora (shark sucker) — despite looking nothing alike, they share a common ancestor.

name: Great Barracuda
habitat: Open water, reef edges, surface waters
diet: Carnivore — fishes, cephalopods and sometimes shrimps
behavior: Solitary ambush predator — can reach speeds of 27mph. Juveniles found in sheltered reefs. Maturity at 66cm. Females release 5,000–30,000 eggs.
funFact: Can accelerate to 27mph in a burst — faster than most sports cars relative to body length, making it one of the ocean's fastest ambush predators.

name: Bull Shark
habitat: Coastal waters, occasionally near deep reef structures
diet: Carnivore — fish, rays, smaller sharks, dolphins
behavior: Rare visitor to Koh Tao. Appears occasionally at deep sites. Known for ability to tolerate freshwater.
funFact: One of the few shark species that can survive in both salt and freshwater. Sightings at Koh Tao are rare and exciting events.

name: Blue-spotted Ribbontail Ray
habitat: Sandy bottoms near coral reefs, under ledges
diet: Carnivore — molluscs, worms, shrimp and crabs
behavior: Bottom dwelling — seeks shelter under coral and rock by day. Nocturnal hunter. Venomous. Males sexually mature when disc reaches 20cm. Can give birth to up to 7 young.
funFact: Gives birth to live young that develop inside egg sacs (ovoviviparous) — can have up to 7 pups in a single litter.

name: Jenkins' Whipray
habitat: Coastal and brackish waters, sandy and silty substrates
diet: Carnivore — mainly small bony fish and crustaceans
behavior: Ovoviviparous. Found in coastal and brackish waters. Males reach sexual maturity at 75–85cm.
funFact: Can tolerate brackish (mixed salt and fresh) water — one of the more adaptable ray species found around Koh Tao.

name: Kuhl's Stingray
habitat: Sandy areas, seagrass, near coral rubble
diet: Carnivore — small crabs, shrimp, worms
behavior: Small and docile. Often found in groups on sandy patches. Buries in sand for camouflage.
funFact: One of the smallest stingray species — fits in the palm of your hand. Their blue spots glow faintly under UV light.

name: Fimbriated Moray
habitat: Sandy and rubble areas, reef edges, under debris
diet: Carnivore — small fish, octopus, crustaceans
behavior: More active during day than most morays. Often found free-swimming across sand. Yellow-green coloring with dark spots.
funFact: Unlike most moray eels that hide in crevices, fimbriated morays are often found swimming freely in the open, making them easier to photograph.

name: Giant Clam
habitat: Shallow coral reefs, well-lit areas
diet: Filter feeder + photosynthesis via symbiotic algae (zooxanthellae)
behavior: Sessile — stays in one place for life. Closes shell slowly when shadows pass over. Hosts symbiotic algae in its mantle tissue.
funFact: Gets most of its nutrition from sunlight via algae living in its tissues, like a solar-powered animal. Can live over 100 years and reach 1.2 meters.

name: Banded Boxer Shrimp
habitat: Reef crevices, caves, and overhangs
diet: Omnivore — parasites, dead skin, small organisms from fish clients
behavior: Cleaning station operator — waves long white antennae to attract fish, then picks parasites from them. Always found in mated pairs.
funFact: They set up 'cleaning stations' where fish line up to get parasites removed. It's like an underwater spa! They wave their antennae like a barber's pole to advertise.

name: Peacock Mantis Shrimp
habitat: Sandy and rubble bottoms, coral reef edges
diet: Carnivore — snails, clams, crabs, other crustaceans
behavior: Punches prey with specialized clubs at acceleration of 10,400g. Creates burrows in sandy substrate. Highly intelligent with complex social behavior.
funFact: Has the fastest punch in the animal kingdom — strikes at the speed of a .22 caliber bullet, creating cavitation bubbles that produce a second shockwave. Can see 16 types of color receptors (humans have 3).

name: Bluefin Trevally
habitat: Reef edges, channels, open water near reef
diet: Carnivore — fish, octopus, crustaceans
behavior: Fast, aggressive hunter. Often seen hunting cooperatively in small groups. Follows larger fish like groupers to catch flushed prey.
funFact: Known for 'shadow hunting' — follows larger predators and catches small fish that flee from the bigger predator's path.
